ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 3, 55, 19, 802643, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Contact`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL

- `legacy_customer_id__c` text NULL

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact  (244 rows)

## Installed_Asset__c

- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 3, 55, 19, 802643, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Contact`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:

- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. **Key mappings:**
- Source contact `id` = "CON-XXXXX" → Target Contact.Id needs transformation. Based on common patterns, likely "SFDC-XXXXX" (preserving the same number format)
- Source account `id` = "CUST-1001" → Target Account.Id likely "0011001..." (Salesforce 15-char Id from the number part)

- Contact.AccountId in source = "CUST-1001" 
- This references an Account with `legacy_customer_id__c` = "CUST-1001"
- Target Account.Id needs to be computed from this

For Account.Id generation (assuming standard approach): the number part of "CUST-XXXXX" gets used. If Account.Id follows a pattern where Account records use something like 'SFDC-' prefix or a Salesforce-style Id, I need to be consistent.

Given the guidelines mention "Salesforce-style Account Id", and looking at the typical pattern:
- Contact.id = "CON-00001" → Contact.Id = SFDC + number part? Or maybe 003 prefix + padded number?
- Account.id = "CUST-1001" → Account.Id = 001 prefix + padded number?

Let me think about the simplest consistent approach that would make joins work:
- If Account.Id is generated from source account.id (e.g., "CUST-1001" → extract 1001 → pad to 8 digits → "00100001001")
- Then Contact.AccountId should map source contact.accountid the same way

But actually, I realize I might be overcomplicating this. Let me look at what's most reasonable:

Looking at the data patterns more carefully:
- Source account.id = "CUST-1001" through "CUST-XXXX" 


- Contact.accountid also references "CUST-XXXX" 

The AccountId in the target is supposed to reference the Salesforce-style Account.Id. So if I generate Account.Id from source account data (e.g., by extracting the numeric portion and applying a standard prefix), then Contact.AccountId should do the same transformation on the matching key.

Since the source already has contact.accountid pointing to "CUST-XXXX", I need to transform this into the same Salesforce-style format used for Account.Id. The cleanest approach would be extracting the number from "CUST-1001" and padding it consistently—so both tables produce matching Ids when joined on this field.

For the Contact model, I'll generate Contact.Id from the source id using the same pattern, apply a CASE statement to map Role__c values (converting German terms like "Entscheider" to "Decision Maker"), normalize Preferred_Language__c into standard ISO codes, and keep Legacy_Contact_ID__c as the original source id.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 3, 55, 19, 802643, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Contact`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.

Key transformations:
1. **Id**: Transform source "CON-XXXXX" to a Salesforce-style Id. Common pattern would be adding prefix or keeping as-is with transformation. Since Account records use similar pattern, let's use "SFDC-" prefix for consistency with what seems like a typical approach.

- Source contact.accountid = "CUST-1001" (raw customer number)  
- Target Contact.AccountId should reference what the Account model generates for its Id
- The Account model needs to transform its source id ("CUST-1001") into a Salesforce-style format

A common approach would be: extract numeric part, pad, and add prefix. E.g.:
- "CUST-1001" → Account.Id = '001' || LPAD('1001', 8, '0') = '001000001001' (Salesforce 15-char pattern: 3-char prefix + 8-char number)

Then Contact.AccountId from source "CUST-1001" would also be transformed the same way.

For Contact.Id from "CON-00001":
- Extract numeric part '00001' → Contact.Id = '003' || LPAD('00001', 8, '0') = '00300000001' ... but that's only 12 chars.

Actually, Salesforce Ids are:
- 15 characters: prefix (usually 3 for object type) + numeric part padded to 12 characters
- Or just 15 characters total

Wait, the standard Salesforce Id format is:
- First 3 characters indicate the object type (001 = Account, 003 = Contact)
- Next 9 characters are unique identifiers

But let me not overthink this. Let me look at a simpler approach:

If Account.id goes from "CUST-1001" to some Id, and Contact.accountid references the same account, then I need consistency. Let me use a simple but consistent transformation:

**Approach**: Use "SFDC-" prefix with the number portion padded appropriately.
- Source "CUST-1001" → Id = 'SFDC-' || LPAD('1001', 8, '0') = 'SFDC-00001001' (15 chars)
- Source "CON-00001" → Id = 'SFDC-' || LPAD('00001', 8, '0') = 'SFDC-00000001' (15 chars)

Then Contact.AccountId from source accountid "CUST-1001" → 'SFDC-00001001' which matches Account.Id for that account.

This is clean and consistent!

Now for the enum mappings:

**Role__c mapping** (case-insensitive, German/English mixed):
- "Entscheider", "Decision Maker" → 'Decision Maker'
- "End User", "Endanwender", "end user" → 'End User'  
- "Technical Contact", "Technischer Ansprechpartner", "Techniker" → 'Technical Contact'
- "Executive Sponsor", "Sponsor" → 'Executive Sponsor'
- Other/NULL → NULL

**Preferred_Language__c mapping**:
- "DE", "de", "Deutsch", "deutsch", "German", "German" → 'DE'
- "EN", "en", "English", "Englisch", "englisch" → 'EN'  
- "FR", "fr", "French", "Französisch", "french" → 'FR'
- "ES" or similar → 'ES'
- "IT" or similar → 'IT'

For contacts I saw: DE, Deutsch, EN, Englis..., English, FR, Franzö..., French, German, de, deutsch, en, englisch, fr

So the mapping is:
- Contains 'd' (Deutsch/deutsch/german/German) → 'DE'
- Contains 'en' or 'englis' → 'EN'
- Contains 'fr' (Franzö/French/fr/french) → 'FR'

Let me write a clean CASE statement.

For Contact:
- Id = 'SFDC-' || LPAD(SUBSTRING(id FROM '\d+'), 8, '0')
- FirstName = INITCAP(TRIM(firstname))

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 3, 55, 50, 428004, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Contact`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.

**Key mappings:**
- Source contact `id` = "CON-XXXXX" → Target Contact.Id needs transformation. Based on common patterns, likely "SFDC-XXXXX" (preserving the same number format)
- Source account `id` = "CUST-1001" → Target Account.Id likely "0011001..." (Salesforce 15-char Id from the number part)

But wait - I should focus on making the Contact model work. The key insight is:
- Contact.AccountId in source = "CUST-1001" 
- This references an Account with `legacy_customer_id__c` = "CUST-1001"
- Target Account.Id needs to be computed from this

For Account.Id generation (assuming standard approach): the number part of "CUST-XXXXX" gets used. If Account.Id follows a pattern where Account records use something like 'SFDC-' prefix or a Salesforce-style Id, I need to be consistent.

Given the guidelines mention "Salesforce-style Account Id", and looking at the typical pattern:
- Contact.id = "CON-00001" → Contact.Id = SFDC + number part? Or maybe 003 prefix + padded number?
- Account.id = "CUST-1001" → Account.Id = 001 prefix + padded number?

Let me think about the simplest consistent approach that would make joins work:
- If Account.Id is generated from source account.id (e.g., "CUST-1001" → extract 1001 → pad to 8 digits → "00100001001")
- Then Contact.AccountId should map source contact.accountid the same way

But actually, I realize I might be overcomplicating this. Let me look at what's most reasonable:

Looking at the data patterns more carefully:
- Source account.id = "CUST-1001" through "CUST-XXXX" 


- Contact.accountid also references "CUST-XXXX" 

The AccountId in the target is supposed to reference the Salesforce-style Account.Id. So if I generate Account.Id from source account data (e.g., by extracting the numeric portion and applying a standard prefix), then Contact.AccountId should do the same transformation on the matching key.

Since the source already has contact.accountid pointing to "CUST-XXXX", I need to transform this into the same Salesforce-style format used for Account.Id. The cleanest approach would be extracting the number from "CUST-1001" and padding it consistently—so both tables produce matching Ids when joined on this field.

For the Contact model, I'll generate Contact.Id from the source id using the same pattern, apply a CASE statement to map Role__c values (converting German terms like "Entscheider" to "Decision Maker"), normalize Preferred_Language__c into standard ISO codes, and keep Legacy_Contact_ID__c as the original source id.

Since CreatedDate, LastModifiedDate, and IsDeleted aren't available in the source data, I'll default them to '1970-01-01', NULL, and 0 respectively. ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 3, 55, 50, 428004, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Contact`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL
- `legacy_customer_id__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact  (244 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `firstname` text NULL

- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.

- Contact.accountid also references "CUST-XXXX" 

The AccountId in the target is supposed to reference the Salesforce-style Account.Id. So if I generate Account.Id from source account data (e.g., by extracting the numeric portion and applying a standard prefix), then Contact.AccountId should do the same transformation on the matching key.

Since the source already has contact.accountid pointing to "CUST-XXXX", I need to transform this into the same Salesforce-style format used for Account.Id. The cleanest approach would be extracting the number from "CUST-1001" and padding it consistently—so both tables produce matching Ids when joined on this field.

For the Contact model, I'll generate Contact.Id from the source id using the same pattern, apply a CASE statement to map Role__c values (converting German terms like "Entscheider" to "Decision Maker"), normalize Preferred_Language__c into standard ISO codes, and keep Legacy_Contact_ID__c as the original source id.

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='I now have enough information. Let me build the Contact model. 

Key transformations:
1. **Id**: Transform source "CON-XXXXX" to a Salesforce-style Id. Common pattern would be adding prefix or keeping as-is with transformation. Since Account records use similar pattern, let's use "SFDC-" prefix for consistency with what seems like a typical approach.

Actually wait - I need to think about this more carefully. The guideline says "AccountId and Account__c must reference the Salesforce-style Account Id, not raw source customer numbers." This implies:
- Source contact.accountid = "CUST-1001" (raw customer number)  
- Target Contact.AccountId should reference what the Account model generates for its Id
- The Account model needs to transform its source id ("CUST-1001") into a Salesforce-style format

A common approach would be: extract numeric part, pad, and add prefix. E.g.:
- "CUST-1001" → Account.Id = '001' || LPAD('1001', 8, '0') = '001000001001' (Salesforce 15-char pattern: 3-char prefix + 8-char number)

Then Contact.AccountId from source "CUST-1001" would also be transformed the same way.

For Contact.Id from "CON-00001":
- Extract numeric part '00001' → Contact.Id = '003' || LPAD('00001', 8, '0') = '00300000001' ... but that's only 12 chars.

Actually, Salesforce Ids are:
- 15 characters: prefix (usually 3 for object type) + numeric part padded to 12 characters
- Or just 15 characters total

Wait, the standard Salesforce Id format is:
- First 3 characters indicate the object type (001 = Account, 003 = Contact)
- Next 9 characters are unique identifiers

But let me not overthink this. Let me look at a simpler approach:

If Account.id goes from "CUST-1001" to some Id, and Contact.accountid references the same account, then I need consistency. Let me use a simple but consistent transformation:

**Approach**: Use "SFDC-" prefix with the number portion padded appropriately.
- Source "CUST-1001" → Id = 'SFDC-' || LPAD('1001', 8, '0') = 'SFDC-00001001' (15 chars)
- Source "CON-00001" → Id = 'SFDC-' || LPAD('00001', 8, '0') = 'SFDC-00000001' (15 chars)

Then Contact.AccountId from source accountid "CUST-1001" → 'SFDC-00001001' which matches Account.Id for that account.

This is clean and consistent!

Now for the enum mappings:

**Role__c mapping** (case-insensitive, German/English mixed):
- "Entscheider", "Decision Maker" → 'Decision Maker'
- "End User", "Endanwender", "end user" → 'End User'  
- "Technical Contact", "Technischer Ansprechpartner", "Techniker" → 'Technical Contact'
- "Executive Sponsor", "Sponsor" → 'Executive Sponsor'
- Other/NULL → NULL

**Preferred_Language__c mapping**:
- "DE", "de", "Deutsch", "deutsch", "German", "German" → 'DE'
- "EN", "en", "English", "Englisch", "englisch" → 'EN'  
- "FR", "fr", "French", "Französisch", "french" → 'FR'
- "ES" or similar → 'ES'
- "IT" or similar → 'IT'

For contacts I saw: DE, Deutsch, EN, Englis..., English, FR, Franzö..., French, German, de, deutsch, en, englisch, fr

So the mapping is:
- Contains 'd' (Deutsch/deutsch/german/German) → 'DE'
- Contains 'en' or 'englis' → 'EN'
- Contains 'fr' (Franzö/French/fr/french) → 'FR'

Let me write a clean CASE statement.

For Contact:
- Id = 'SFDC-' || LPAD(SUBSTRING(id FROM '\d+'), 8, '0')
- FirstName = INITCAP(TRIM(firstname))
- LastName = INITCAP(TRIM(lastname)) — NOT NULL in target, so use COALESCE/NULLIF
- Email = CASE WHEN email IN (NULL, '', 'N/A') THEN NULL ELSE UPPER(TRIM(email)) END
- Phone = phone (keep as is, possibly TRIM)
- Title = INITCAP(TRIM(title))
- Role__c = mapped CASE
- Preferred_Language__c = mapped CASE
- AccountId = 'SFDC-' || LPAD(SUBSTRING(accountid FROM '\d+'), 8, '0')
- Legacy_Contact_ID__c = id (source key)
- CreatedDate = NULL or default
- LastModifiedDate = NULL or default  
- IsDeleted = 0

Wait - let me reconsider the Id format. The guideline says "AccountId and Account__c must reference the Salesforce-style Account Id". Let me think about what format makes sense for both source patterns:

Actually, I realize there might be an even simpler approach. What if the pattern is just using the numeric part with consistent padding?

Source contact id like "CON-00001" → The number portion is 5 digits
Source account id like "CUST-1001" → The number portion varies (1001 to ~1108)

If I extract the number and pad: 'SFDC-' || LPAD(numeric, 8, '0') works for both.

Let me finalize this approach and write the SQL.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='query_postgres', args='{"sql": "SELECT DISTINCT role__c FROM \"fixture_messy_data_v2_src\".\"contact\" WHERE role__c IS NOT NULL AND role__c != '' ORDER BY role__c"}', tool_call_id='call_kt0yv9y9'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='query_postgres', args='{"sql": "SELECT DISTINCT preferred_language__c FROM \"fixture_messy_data_v2_src\".\"contact\" WHERE preferred_language__c IS NOT NULL AND preferred_language__c != '' ORDER BY preferred_language__c"}', tool_call_id='call_bgj7ssx4')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=9136, output_tokens=1483), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 3, 56, 25, 879863, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 3, 37, 9, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-85b8b67e-f491-4811-9cfb-b9008cf06d19', finish_reason='tool_call')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 3, 55, 50, 428004, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Contact`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL
- `legacy_customer_id__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact  (244 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `firstname` text NULL
- `lastname` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `phone` text NULL
- `title` text NULL
- `role__c` text NULL
- `preferred_language__c` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.installed_asset__c  (480 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial_number__c` text NULL
- `warranty_end_date__c` text NULL

- `project__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity  (303 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stagename` text NULL
- `closedate` text NULL
- `amount` text NULL
- `currencyisocode` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.project__c  (190 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL

- `project_status__c` text NULL
- `go_live_date__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)

## Contact

## Opportunity

- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text

- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c

- `Go_Live_Date__c` text

- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text

For the Contact model, I'll generate Contact.Id from the source id using the same pattern, apply a CASE statement to map Role__c values (converting German terms like "Entscheider" to "Decision Maker"), normalize Preferred_Language__c into standard ISO codes, and keep Legacy_Contact_ID__c as the original source id.

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='I now have enough information. Let me build the Contact model. 

Key transformations:
1. **Id**: Transform source "CON-XXXXX" to a Salesforce-style Id. Common pattern would be adding prefix or keeping as-is with transformation. Since Account records use similar pattern, let's use "SFDC-" prefix for consistency with what seems like a typical approach.

Actually wait - I need to think about this more carefully. The guideline says "AccountId and Account__c must reference the Salesforce-style Account Id, not raw source customer numbers." This implies:
- Source contact.accountid = "CUST-1001" (raw customer number)  
- Target Contact.AccountId should reference what the Account model generates for its Id
- The Account model needs to transform its source id ("CUST-1001") into a Salesforce-style format

A common approach would be: extract numeric part, pad, and add prefix. E.g.:
- "CUST-1001" → Account.Id = '001' || LPAD('1001', 8, '0') = '001000001001' (Salesforce 15-char pattern: 3-char prefix + 8-char number)

Then Contact.AccountId from source "CUST-1001" would also be transformed the same way.

For Contact.Id from "CON-00001":
- Extract numeric part '00001' → Contact.Id = '003' || LPAD('00001', 8, '0') = '00300000001' ... but that's only 12 chars.

Actually, Salesforce Ids are:
- 15 characters: prefix (usually 3 for object type) + numeric part padded to 12 characters
- Or just 15 characters total

Wait, the standard Salesforce Id format is:
- First 3 characters indicate the object type (001 = Account, 003 = Contact)
- Next 9 characters are unique identifiers

But let me not overthink this. Let me look at a simpler approach:

If Account.id goes from "CUST-1001" to some Id, and Contact.accountid references the same account, then I need consistency. Let me use a simple but consistent transformation:

**Approach**: Use "SFDC-" prefix with the number portion padded appropriately.
- Source "CUST-1001" → Id = 'SFDC-' || LPAD('1001', 8, '0') = 'SFDC-00001001' (15 chars)
- Source "CON-00001" → Id = 'SFDC-' || LPAD('00001', 8, '0') = 'SFDC-00000001' (15 chars)

Then Contact.AccountId from source accountid "CUST-1001" → 'SFDC-00001001' which matches Account.Id for that account.

This is clean and consistent!

Now for the enum mappings:

**Role__c mapping** (case-insensitive, German/English mixed):
- "Entscheider", "Decision Maker" → 'Decision Maker'
- "End User", "Endanwender", "end user" → 'End User'  
- "Technical Contact", "Technischer Ansprechpartner", "Techniker" → 'Technical Contact'
- "Executive Sponsor", "Sponsor" → 'Executive Sponsor'
- Other/NULL → NULL

**Preferred_Language__c mapping**:
- "DE", "de", "Deutsch", "deutsch", "German", "German" → 'DE'
- "EN", "en", "English", "Englisch", "englisch" → 'EN'  
- "FR", "fr", "French", "Französisch", "french" → 'FR'
- "ES" or similar → 'ES'
- "IT" or similar → 'IT'

For contacts I saw: DE, Deutsch, EN, Englis..., English, FR, Franzö..., French, German, de, deutsch, en, englisch, fr

So the mapping is:
- Contains 'd' (Deutsch/deutsch/german/German) → 'DE'
- Contains 'en' or 'englis' → 'EN'
- Contains 'fr' (Franzö/French/fr/french) → 'FR'

Let me write a clean CASE statement.

For Contact:
- Id = 'SFDC-' || LPAD(SUBSTRING(id FROM '\d+'), 8, '0')
- FirstName = INITCAP(TRIM(firstname))
- LastName = INITCAP(TRIM(lastname)) — NOT NULL in target, so use COALESCE/NULLIF
- Email = CASE WHEN email IN (NULL, '', 'N/A') THEN NULL ELSE UPPER(TRIM(email)) END
- Phone = phone (keep as is, possibly TRIM)
- Title = INITCAP(TRIM(title))
- Role__c = mapped CASE
- Preferred_Language__c = mapped CASE
- AccountId = 'SFDC-' || LPAD(SUBSTRING(accountid FROM '\d+'), 8, '0')
- Legacy_Contact_ID__c = id (source key)
- CreatedDate = NULL or default
- LastModifiedDate = NULL or default  
- IsDeleted = 0

Wait - let me reconsider the Id format. The guideline says "AccountId and Account__c must reference the Salesforce-style Account Id". Let me think about what format makes sense for both source patterns:

Actually, I realize there might be an even simpler approach. What if the pattern is just using the numeric part with consistent padding?

Source contact id like "CON-00001" → The number portion is 5 digits
Source account id like "CUST-1001" → The number portion varies (1001 to ~1108)

If I extract the number and pad: 'SFDC-' || LPAD(numeric, 8, '0') works for both.

Let me finalize this approach and write the SQL.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='query_postgres', args='{"sql": "SELECT DISTINCT role__c FROM \"fixture_messy_data_v2_src\".\"contact\" WHERE role__c IS NOT NULL AND role__c != '' ORDER BY role__c"}', tool_call_id='call_kt0yv9y9'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='query_postgres', args='{"sql": "SELECT DISTINCT preferred_language__c FROM \"fixture_messy_data_v2_src\".\"contact\" WHERE preferred_language__c IS NOT NULL AND preferred_language__c != '' ORDER BY preferred_language__c"}', tool_call_id='call_bgj7ssx4')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=9136, output_tokens=1483), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 3, 56, 25, 879863, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 9, 3, 37, 9, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-85b8b67e-f491-4811-9cfb-b9008cf06d19', finish_reason='tool_call', run_id='019f4504-7f1a-749a-8484-9bdb68439223', conversation_id='019f4504-7f1a-749a-8484-9bda27a2ae06')

- Legacy_Contact_ID__c = source id

**Enum mappings:**

Role__c:
- "Decision Maker", "decision maker" → 'Decision Maker'
- "End User", "end user", "Endanwender" → 'End User'
- "Technical Contact", "Technischer Ansprechpartner", "Techniker" → 'Technical Contact'
- "Executive Sponsor", "Sponsor" → 'Executive Sponsor'
- "Entscheider" → 'Decision Maker' (German)
- NULL, empty, "N/A" → NULL

Preferred_Language__c:
- "DE", "de", "Deutsch", "deutsch", "German" → 'DE'
- "EN", "en", "English", "Englisch", "englisch" → 'EN'
- "FR", "fr", "French", "Französisch", "french" → 'FR'
- NULL, empty → NULL

**Other fields:**
- FirstName: INITCAP(TRIM(firstname)) 
- LastName: INITCAP(TRIM(lastname)), but NOT NULL constraint means I should COALESCE or handle NULLs
- Email: CASE WHEN email IS NULL OR email = '' OR email = 'N/A' THEN NULL ELSE UPPER(TRIM(email)) END
- Phone: TRIM(phone)
- Title: INITCAP(TRIM(title))
- IsDeleted = 0
- CreatedDate, LastModifiedDate: Since source doesn't have these, use NULL or defaults. The target says text type with no NOT NULL, so NULL is fine.

Actually wait - I need to check if LastName has a NOT NULL constraint. Looking at the target schema: "LastName text NOT NULL". So I must ensure every row has a non-null LastName. I'll use COALESCE(TRIM(lastname), 'Unknown') or similar.

But actually, looking at the sample data, all contacts seem to have a lastname. Let me still add a safety COALESCE.

Let me write the final SQL now.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), TextPart(content='Now I have all the information needed. c.email IS NULL OR TRIM(c.email) = '' OR TRIM(UPPER(c.email)) = 'N/A' THEN NULL\n        ELSE UPPER(TRIM(c.email))\n    END AS \"Email\",\n    TRIM(c.phone) AS \"Phone\",\n    INITCAP(TRIM(c.title)) AS \"Title\",\n    CASE\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(COALESCE(c.role__c, ''))) IN ('decision maker', 'entscheider') THEN 'Decision Maker'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(COALESCE(c.role__c, ''))) IN ('end user', 'endanwender') THEN 'End User'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(COALESCE(c.role__c, ''))) IN ('technical contact', 'technischer ansprechpartner', 'techniker') THEN 'Technical Contact'\n        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(COALESCE(c.role__c, ''))) IN ('executive sponsor', 'sponsor') THEN 'Executive Sponsor'\n        ELSE NULL\n    END AS \"Role__c\",\n    CASE\n        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(COALESCE(c.preferred_language__c, ''))) IN ('DE', 'DEUTSCH', 'DEUTSCH') THEN 'DE'\n        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(COALESCE(c.preferred_language__c, ''))) IN ('EN', 'ENGLISCH', 'ENGLISH', '0')\n        ELSE NULL\n    END AS \"AccountId\",\n    c.id AS \"Legacy_Contact_ID__c\",\n    NULL AS \"CreatedDate\",\n    NULL AS \"LastModifiedDate\",\n    0 AS \"IsDeleted\"\n\nFROM {{
